event: The Death of Agag — Samuel executes the Amalekite king
symbols: Samuel with sword over Agag — prophetic authority executing divine judgment
kneeling Agag — the defeated enemy of God brought low
army of soldiers with spears — Israel as God's instrument of judgment
Samuel's robes versus Agag's humbled posture — spiritual authority over worldly kingship
description: A detailed black and white engraving showing a robed prophetic figure (Samuel) standing over a kneeling man (King Agag) in a commanding posture, apparently about to execute him with a sword. Behind them, a dense formation of Israelite soldiers holding upright spears fills the background, creating a wall of military force. The figures in the foreground are rendered with dramatic detail — Samuel in flowing priestly robes, Agag in a posture of defeat. The scene conveys the gravity and finality of the execution. Text below identifies it as 'Death of Agag' with a biblical citation.
theological_description: The death of Agag (1 Samuel 15:32-33) represents one of the most sobering episodes of divine judgment in the Old Testament. God had commanded Saul to utterly destroy the Amalekites and their king as judgment for their attack on Israel during the Exodus (1 Samuel 15:2-3; cf. Exodus 17:8-16; Deuteronomy 25:17-19). When Saul disobediently spared Agag and the best livestock, Samuel confronted him with the words that became a foundational principle of biblical theology: 'Has the Lord as great delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices, as in obeying the voice of the Lord? Behold, to obey is better than sacrifice' (1 Samuel 15:22). Samuel then executed Agag himself, declaring: 'As your sword has made women childless, so shall your mother be childless among women' (1 Samuel 15:33). This episode marks the turning point of Saul's kingship — his disobedience led to his rejection by God and the eventual anointing of David. The theological lesson is stark: God's commands are not subject to human revision, and partial obedience is disobedience.

scripture_refs.reason: Samuel's execution of King Agag — the event directly depicted
To obey is better than sacrifice — the theological principle established through Saul's failure
God's command to destroy the Amalekites — the divine order that precipitated the events
The Amalekites' original attack on Israel — the historical basis for God's judgment
The command to blot out the memory of Amalek — the Deuteronomic mandate Saul failed to execute
